questions.text: What are Custom Agents in Kiro?

options.label: Configurable specialized agents that maintain their own context and can be optimized for specific domains
Automation scripts that run in the background without user interaction
Third-party extensions installed via the editor's marketplace
Saved prompt templates for reuse in future conversations

questions.explanation: Custom Agents are configurable specialized agents that maintain their own context and can be optimized for specific domains like frontend, backend, code review, and more. Each agent has its own system prompt and settings.

questions.text: Custom Agents share the same context as the main chat session.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Each Custom Agent maintains its own isolated context. This is one of the main advantages of Custom Agents: avoiding context pollution between different domains, keeping each agent focused on its area of specialization.

questions.text: Where are Custom Agents configured in a Kiro project?

options.label: In the editor's settings.json file
In the .kiro/agents/ directory of the project, in configuration files
In the Kiro global preferences panel
In the project's package.json file, in the 'kiro' section

questions.explanation: Custom Agents are configured in the .kiro/agents/ directory of the project. Each agent has its own configuration file that defines its system prompt, available tools, and scope of action.

questions.text: Your team has a React frontend and a Python backend. The agent's context is overloaded with tools from both. How to solve this?

options.label: Increase the context token limit in settings
Create separate subagents: a frontend-agent with browser/component tools and a backend-agent with database/API docs
Manually switch between different projects in the editor
Use .kiroignore to hide backend files when working on frontend

questions.explanation: The ideal solution is to create separate subagents: a frontend-agent optimized with browser tools and React components, and a backend-agent with database access and API documentation. Each agent maintains isolated context, avoiding overload.

questions.text: What is the main difference between Custom Agents, Steering Files, and Skills in Kiro?

options.label: There is no difference, they are just different names for the same feature
Steering = always-active rules; Skills = on-demand knowledge; Custom Agents = complete agents with isolated context and own tools
Skills are for frontend, Steering for backend, and Custom Agents for DevOps
Custom Agents completely replace Steering Files and Skills when configured

questions.explanation: Each concept has a distinct role: Steering Files define rules that are always active in every interaction; Skills are knowledge packages activated on demand when needed; Custom Agents are complete agents with isolated context, own tools, and dedicated system prompt.

questions.text: You want to create a code review agent that validates security standards automatically. Which approach is most appropriate?

options.label: Add security rules in the project's global steering file
Create a Custom Agent with a security-focused system prompt, configured in .kiro/agents/
Install a security linting extension in the editor
Create a hook that runs a security scanner after each commit

questions.explanation: The most appropriate approach is to create a dedicated Custom Agent for security code review, configured in .kiro/agents/. This agent will have a system prompt focused on security standards, with isolated context and specific tools for vulnerability analysis.
